disclaimer: ALL content in this file is generated by AI for audit preparation assistance only. It has NO regulatory authority. It must NEVER be presented to auditors as regulatory text or official ECC requirements. Official compliance determinations must reference Layer 1 verbatim text from ECC-2:2024.

audit_questions: Are cybersecurity requirements for business continuity management (BCM) formally identified, documented, and approved?
Do the requirements address continuity of cybersecurity systems, cyber-incident response within BCM, and disaster recovery — all three minimum items in 3-1-3?
Is the document approved by the Authorized Official?
Are BCM cybersecurity requirements aligned with the entity's broader BCM and BCP framework?
suggested_evidence: BCM cybersecurity requirements document with Authorized Official approval signature
Scope statement covering all three minimum requirement areas (continuity of cybersecurity systems, cyber-incident response within BCM, and disaster recovery)
Cross-reference or linkage to the entity's overarching BCP/BCM policy
indicators_of_implementation: Approved requirements document exists, is current, and references all three minimum areas
Cybersecurity requirements are integrated into the entity's formal BCM framework — not maintained as an isolated document
Document covers both prevention of disruption and recovery from cyber-caused disruption

maturity_signals.initial: No BCM cybersecurity requirements; business continuity planning treats cybersecurity as out of scope
maturity_signals.developing: Requirements documented but not formally approved; only partial coverage of the three minimum areas
maturity_signals.defined: Approved, comprehensive requirements covering all three minimum areas; integrated with entity BCM
maturity_signals.advanced: Requirements aligned with ISO 22301 BCM standard; cyber resilience is a first-class concern in all business continuity planning; reviewed after significant incidents or changes

audit_questions: Are BCM cybersecurity requirements actively implemented — not just documented?
Do operational BCPs and DR plans incorporate all three minimum cybersecurity requirements?
Is there evidence that cybersecurity systems continuity, cyber-incident response within BCM, and DR plans are all operational?
Are BCPs and DR plans tested and updated based on test results?
suggested_evidence: Active Business Continuity Plans (BCPs) containing cybersecurity resilience sections
Disaster recovery plan documentation
BCM test exercise records showing cybersecurity scenarios were included
Cyber-incident response integration into BCM activation records
indicators_of_implementation: BCPs reference and integrate the cybersecurity continuity requirements
All three minimum elements (3-1-3-1 through 3-1-3-3) are implemented — not just planned
BCPs have been tested within the defined review cycle

maturity_signals.initial: BCM exists but cybersecurity requirements are not implemented within it; BCPs do not address cyber threats
maturity_signals.developing: Cybersecurity is mentioned in BCPs but DR plans are absent or untested
maturity_signals.defined: All three minimum requirements are implemented within BCM; tested periodically
maturity_signals.advanced: Automated failover for critical cybersecurity systems; cyber resilience integrated into crisis management; quarterly BCP exercises include cyber attack scenarios

possible_technical_evidence: BCP exercise scenario documentation and results

audit_questions: Do BCM cybersecurity requirements explicitly include all three minimum requirements: cybersecurity systems continuity (3-1-3-1), cyber-incident response plans within BCM (3-1-3-2), and disaster recovery plans (3-1-3-3)?
Are all three minimum requirements implemented and evidenced?
Are the three requirements kept current and aligned with the entity's technology and risk landscape?
suggested_evidence: BCM policy or requirements document listing all three minimum items
Technical and procedural evidence for each subcontrol (3-1-3-1 through 3-1-3-3)
Review records showing the three requirements are kept current
indicators_of_implementation: All three minimum requirements are explicitly referenced in the BCM cybersecurity requirements document
Operational evidence exists for each — plans are written, owned, and tested
No minimum requirement is missing from implemented BCM

maturity_signals.initial: Fewer than two of the three minimum requirements exist
maturity_signals.developing: Two of the three requirements exist; one area (typically DR) is absent or incomplete
maturity_signals.defined: All three requirements are implemented and evidenced
maturity_signals.advanced: All three are automated, tested, and integrated with crisis management and IT service management

audit_questions: Are cybersecurity systems (SIEM, EDR, IAM, firewall, PAM, backups) included in the entity's BCM scope with continuity requirements?
Is there a defined RTO and RPO for each critical cybersecurity system?
Are redundancy or failover mechanisms in place for critical cybersecurity controls?
If a primary cybersecurity system fails during an incident, are manual fallback procedures documented?
suggested_evidence: BCM asset register listing cybersecurity systems with assigned RTO and RPO
High-availability or redundancy configuration for critical cybersecurity tools (SIEM, EDR, firewall)
Manual fallback procedures for critical cybersecurity controls during system outage
BCM test results confirming cybersecurity systems recovered within RTO
indicators_of_implementation: Critical cybersecurity systems are explicitly listed in the BCM asset register with RTO/RPO
Redundancy or failover is configured for critical controls (e.g., dual firewalls, SIEM HA, replicated EDR console)
Fallback procedures exist for scenarios where cybersecurity tools are unavailable during an incident

maturity_signals.initial: Cybersecurity systems are not in scope for BCM; no RTO/RPO assigned; no redundancy
maturity_signals.developing: Some cybersecurity systems are in BCM scope; RTO/RPO undefined or untested
maturity_signals.defined: All critical cybersecurity systems in BCM scope; RTO/RPO defined; redundancy implemented; fallback procedures documented
maturity_signals.advanced: Automated failover with near-zero RTO for Tier 1 cybersecurity systems; failover tested quarterly; cybersecurity system availability monitored 24/7

possible_documents: BCM Asset Register with Cybersecurity Systems
possible_technical_evidence: BCM test results showing cybersecurity system recovery times

audit_questions: Are there documented plans for responding to cybersecurity incidents that may affect the entity's business continuity (e.g., ransomware, extended outage from a cyber attack)?
Are cyber-specific scenarios incorporated into the entity's BCP activation criteria?
Do the plans define escalation, communication, and decision-making authority when a cyber incident triggers a BCP invocation?
Have cyber-incident-triggered BCP scenarios been tested through exercises?
suggested_evidence: BCP sections or annexes specifically addressing cybersecurity incident scenarios (ransomware, DDoS, critical system compromise)
BCP activation criteria listing cybersecurity incident types that trigger BCP invocation
Escalation and communication matrix for cyber-triggered BCP activation
Tabletop or simulation exercise records testing cyber-incident BCP scenarios
indicators_of_implementation: Cyber incident scenarios (at minimum ransomware and extended outage) are explicitly covered in BCP
BCP activation criteria include cybersecurity incident severity thresholds
Exercises have tested at least one cyber-triggered BCP scenario within the last 12 months

maturity_signals.initial: BCPs do not address cyber-caused disruptions; plans assume only physical or natural-disaster causes
maturity_signals.developing: Cyber incidents mentioned in BCP but no specific response procedures; no scenario testing
maturity_signals.defined: Documented cyber-incident BCP scenarios with activation criteria; tested at least annually
maturity_signals.advanced: Cyber-incident BCP scenarios tested with red team simulations; recovery decisions automated where possible; lessons learned feed plan improvements

possible_technical_evidence: Incident response platform integration with BCP activation workflow
BCP exercise simulation results and after-action reports

audit_questions: Are formal Disaster Recovery (DR) plans documented for all critical systems and services?
Do DR plans define RTO, RPO, recovery procedures, responsible parties, and required resources?
Are DR plans tested through periodic DR exercises or failover tests?
Are DR plans kept current with changes to the technology environment?
Is DR infrastructure maintained in a state ready to receive production workloads?
suggested_evidence: Disaster Recovery Plans for all critical systems with defined RTO and RPO
DR test schedule and most recent DR exercise or failover test records
DR test results showing actual recovery time versus RTO targets
DR infrastructure readiness assessment or configuration documentation
DR plan version history showing updates after technology changes
indicators_of_implementation: DR plans exist for every critical system and service — not just IT infrastructure
RTO and RPO are defined, tested, and met within acceptable tolerance
DR has been tested within the defined cycle (at minimum annually) and results are documented
DR infrastructure (standby servers, replication, cloud DR) is configured and maintained

maturity_signals.initial: No DR plans; recovery from disaster is entirely ad-hoc
maturity_signals.developing: DR plans exist for some critical systems; never tested; DR infrastructure informal
maturity_signals.defined: DR plans for all critical systems; RTO/RPO defined and tested annually; DR infrastructure maintained
maturity_signals.advanced: Orchestrated DR automation (Zerto, Azure Site Recovery, AWS DRS); hot/warm standby for Tier 1 systems; DR tested quarterly; lessons learned documented and actioned

possible_documents: Disaster Recovery Plans per Critical System
possible_technical_evidence: DR replication configuration (Veeam Replication, Zerto, Azure Site Recovery)
RTO measurement records from DR exercises

audit_questions: Are BCM cybersecurity requirements reviewed on a defined periodic schedule?
Are reviews triggered by significant incidents, technology changes, or regulatory updates — in addition to scheduled intervals?
Are changes to BCM cybersecurity requirements documented, approved, and communicated to relevant parties?
Is there evidence that the most recent review occurred within the defined cycle?
suggested_evidence: BCM cybersecurity requirements review log with dates, reviewers, and outcomes
Version history of BCM cybersecurity requirements or BCP documents
Post-incident or post-exercise review records leading to BCM updates
Communication records showing updated requirements were shared with BCP owners
indicators_of_implementation: A defined review frequency is documented within the BCM requirements or a governing schedule
The most recent review falls within the defined cycle
Lessons from BCM exercises and cyber incidents feed into requirement updates

maturity_signals.initial: BCM requirements never reviewed after initial creation; plans become stale
maturity_signals.developing: Occasional reviews occur but with no defined schedule; not triggered by incidents
maturity_signals.defined: Periodic review on schedule; incident and exercise findings trigger updates; changes approved
maturity_signals.advanced: Continuous improvement cycle: every exercise generates action items; BCM requirements updated after each major incident; aligned with enterprise risk review cadence

possible_documents: Post-Exercise Action Items and Implementation Records
